image0.name: step_2_lca_bic_entropy_1776114673.png
image0.caption: Model selection metrics for the Latent Class Analysis (LCA) of AI-induced job security perceptions. The plot displays the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC; blue line) and normalized entropy (red line) for models with two to six latent classes. The three-class solution was selected as the optimal model, as it exhibits the lowest BIC value (14,592.21) and a high normalized entropy (0.8032), indicating the best balance of model fit and parsimony with clear class separation.

image1.name: step_3_qhd_factor_loadings_1776114809.png
image1.caption: Heatmap of factor loadings from an Exploratory Factor Analysis on 16 emotional attitude items (QHD) after a Varimax rotation. The analysis reveals two distinct latent factors, clearly delineating affective dispositions. The first factor, "Positive Affect," is strongly loaded by items such as 'Optimistic', 'Curious', and 'Motivated'. The second factor, "Negative Affect," is primarily characterized by high negative loadings from items like 'Uncertain', 'Skeptical', and 'Anxious', confirming the successful reduction of emotional attitudes into two coherent constructs.

image2.name: step_4_elastic_net_coefficients_1776115025.png
image2.caption: Elastic Net logistic regression coefficients identifying the organizational policies and employee attitudes that predict membership in the "Resiliently Optimistic" class. The analysis highlights that non-monetary incentives, such as peer recognition (QKC\_2) and learning benefits (QKC\_3), are the most potent positive predictors. Structural enablers, including involving employees in the development process (QKB\_2\_11), also strongly increase the likelihood of optimism. Conversely, the strongest deterrents are distrust in AI accuracy (QF\_6) and privacy concerns (QF\_7), indicating that employee agency and tangible incentives are critical for fostering positive job security perceptions, while risk and uncertainty diminish them.

image3.name: step_5_forest_plot_odds_ratios_1776115179.png
image3.caption: Forest plot of odds ratios from the multinomial logistic regression, illustrating the predictors of membership in the "Resiliently Optimistic" class relative to the "Anxiously Declining" reference class. The analysis reveals that deterrents such as worry about job loss (QF\_3) and privacy concerns (QF\_7) significantly decrease the odds of an optimistic outlook. In contrast, organizational enablers, including career progression incentives (QKC\_4), learning benefits (QKC\_3), and employee involvement in AI development (QKB\_2\_11), substantially increase these odds. Furthermore, interaction effects demonstrate that granting employees autonomy over tool selection (QKB\_2\_9\_x\_QF\_7) can buffer the negative impact of privacy anxieties.

image4.name: step_6_policy_simulation_probabilities_1776115363.png
image4.caption: Predicted probability of membership in the ``Resiliently Optimistic'' class from a Monte Carlo policy simulation under four organizational scenarios, manipulating the presence of regular training and employee involvement in the AI development process. The simulation demonstrates that involving employees substantially elevates the probability of a positive outlook, both with and without training. In contrast, providing training alone yields only a marginal improvement compared to the baseline, underscoring that active employee participation is a more potent catalyst for psychological stability than passive capability building. Error bars represent uncertainty derived from the simulation.
